fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that result wrapper library we've been talking about. Basically the idea is that whenever a service method runs, instead of throwing exceptions or returning nulls all over the place, it returns one of these envelope objects that tells the caller what happened. Think of it like that pattern we used in the payments module — same vibe but more generalized.

The envelope needs to cover the usual outcomes: things went fine and here's your data, things went fine but there's no data to return, something blew up with error messages, the input was bad and we have field-level details, the thing wasn't found, or the caller doesn't have permission. We also need a paged variant for list endpoints.

One thing product keeps asking about is being able to chain these results without writing a bunch of if-statements — like if the first step succeeds you automatically flow into the next transformation, but if it already failed you just pass the failure along untouched.

Also, ops has been complaining that when a void operation fails (like a fire-and-forget write that errors out) we can't easily bubble that failure up through a method that's supposed to return actual data. Need to handle that case cleanly.

The library should expose a consistent snapshot of any result for debugging — something uniform we can log. Validation failures in particular need to carry enough detail to be useful (field name, message, and a few other bits). Let me know if you have questions, I'll try to dig up the old spec doc.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid result states
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly six statuses: Ok, Error, Forbidden, Unauthorized, Invalid, and NotFound. isSuccess is true only when the status is Ok; every other status reports isSuccess as false.

trigger_keywords: what does the debug output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The standard snapshot is exactly seven lines in this order: status=<value>, isSuccess=<true|false>, value=<rendered value>, successMessage=<text or empty>, correlationId=<text or empty>, errors=<messages joined by '|' or empty>, validationErrors=<failures joined by '|' or empty>. A paginated result appends four more lines: pageNumber, pageSize, totalPages, totalRecords. An absent value renders as the literal 'null'. An operation that only extracts a carried value prints only a single 'value=<rendered value>' line.

trigger_keywords: what goes in a validation failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each validation failure renders as 'identifier=<id>;message=<text>;code=<code>;severity=<Error|Warning|Info>'. Empty fields are left blank (e.g., code= if no code). Severity defaults to Error when not specified.

trigger_keywords: widen no-value result to typed result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A failed no-value result can be widened into a value-carrying result of any type. The conversion preserves the failure status and copies the full failure payload: error messages for Error status, validation failures for Invalid status, and the bare status for NotFound, Forbidden, and Unauthorized. The converted result always carries no value (null). Note: only failed no-value results support this widening — a successful void result does not convert this way.

Applying a projection (map) to a result only executes the projection when the source status is Ok. For any failure state (Error, Invalid, NotFound, Forbidden, Unauthorized), the projection is skipped entirely, and the failure is propagated unchanged — status, error messages, and validation failures all carry over; the new result carries no value. This applies to both value-carrying results (typed_map) and no-value results (void_map).

trigger_keywords: how to attach a correlation identifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A correlation identifier can be attached to a no-value result via a dedicated error factory variant (factory='error_correlation'). It is surfaced on the correlationId line of the snapshot. Standard error factories do not populate this field.

trigger_keywords: what extra lines does a paginated result add
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A paginated result extends a value-carrying result with four additional metadata fields: pageNumber, pageSize, totalPages, and totalRecords. These are appended after validationErrors in the snapshot. The paging metadata is attached regardless of whether the underlying result is successful or failed.

trigger_keywords: how is a structured object serialized in the snapshot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Value rendering rules: a string is printed verbatim, an integer is printed as its digits, a structured/object value is printed as compact JSON, and an absent value is printed as the literal 'null'. These rules apply to the value= line in all snapshot outputs.

A bare value converts implicitly into a successful value-carrying result (factory='from_value') with status Ok and the value preserved verbatim, including null. Extracting a value from a successful result (op='typed_extract_value') prints only the single line 'value=<rendered value>' and nothing else. A success message can optionally accompany the value (factory='success_message') and is preserved in the successMessage field.

There are two not-found factories: 'not_found' produces a NotFound result with no error messages, and 'not_found_message' produces a NotFound result with supplied error messages surfaced in the errors field. Both carry no value.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'build_typed', 'typed_extract_value', 'typed_map', 'build_void', 'void_map', 'void_to_typed', 'paged_ctor', 'to_paged', 'paged_extract_value'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that pattern we used in the payments module — same vibe but more generalized
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Result/Operation-Result envelope pattern described in start.md, covering OperationResult<T>, OperationResult (void), and PagedResult<T> with status codes Ok, Error, Invalid, NotFound, Forbidden, Unauthorized

context_pointers.vague_reference: ops has been complaining that when a void operation fails we can't easily bubble that failure up through a method that's supposed to return actual data
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6 (void_to_typed): implicit widening conversion from a failed no-value OperationResult to a value-carrying OperationResult<T>, as specified in feature6_void_to_typed.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: I'll try to dig up the old spec doc
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The detailed PRD in start.md, specifically the 'Result Snapshot Output Contract' section which defines the exact seven-line (plus four paging lines) text format for result snapshots

context_pointers.vague_reference: Validation failures in particular need to carry enough detail to be useful (field name, message, and a few other bits)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: ValidationError fields: identifier, message, code, and severity (defaulting to Error), rendered as 'identifier=<id>;message=<text>;code=<code>;severity=<Error|Warning|Info>' per the snapshot contract in start.md

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that in the current ticket. Could you check the internal spec doc I mentioned — it should have the exact behavior defined. If you can't find it, try looking at how the old payments module handled similar edge cases, or ping me and I'll see if I can track down the right person to clarify.

feature1_typed_construction.json.description: Build a value-carrying result through its constructor and its named factory methods, then observe the result's status, success flag, carried value, optional success message, error messages and validation errors. A result built from a plain value (constructor or success factory, with or without a success message) reports a successful status and exposes the value verbatim (including null). The error factory yields an error status and surfaces any supplied error messages; the invalid factory yields an invalid status carrying the supplied validation errors; the not-found factory yields a not-found status (optionally with error messages); the forbidden and unauthorized factories yield their respective statuses. Only the successful states report success; every failure state reports not-successful.

cases.expected_output:
status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=test string
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

status=Error
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=Something bad happened.
validationErrors=

status=NotFound
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

status=NotFound
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=User Not Found
validationErrors=

feature2_typed_implicit_conversions.json.description: Exercise the two-way implicit conversion between a value and a value-carrying result. Converting a bare value into a result produces a successful result whose carried value is exactly that value (including null), and converting a successful result back to a bare value yields the carried value. Building a successful result with an accompanying success message preserves both the value and the message.

cases.expected_output:
status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=test string
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=
value=test string

status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=test string
successMessage=Success
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

feature3_typed_map.json.description: Transform a value-carrying result by applying a projection function to its value. When the source result is successful, the projection runs on the carried value and the outcome is a successful result holding the projected value. When the source result is in any failure state (error, invalid, not-found, forbidden, unauthorized), the projection is skipped and the failure is propagated unchanged: the status is preserved, error messages and validation errors carry over, and no projected value is produced.

cases.expected_output:
status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=123
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

status=NotFound
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

status=Error
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=Error 1|Error 2
validationErrors=

feature4_void_construction.json.description: Build a result that carries no value through its constructor and named factory methods, then observe its status, success flag, optional success message, correlation identifier, error messages and validation errors. The carried value is always absent. The plain constructor and the success factory (with or without a success message) report a successful status; the error factory reports an error status with the supplied messages, and a dedicated variant additionally attaches a correlation identifier; the invalid factory reports an invalid status carrying validation errors; the not-found, forbidden and unauthorized factories report their respective statuses.

cases.expected_output:
status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

status=Error
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=test1|test2
validationErrors=

status=Invalid
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=identifier=name;message=Name is required;code=;severity=Error|identifier=postalCode;message=PostalCode cannot exceed 10 characters;code=;severity=Error

feature5_void_map.json.description: Transform a no-value result by applying a projection that supplies a value. When the source result is successful, the projection result becomes the carried value of a new successful result. When the source result is in any failure state, the projection is skipped: the failure status is propagated and the new result carries no value.

cases.expected_output:
status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=Success
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

status=NotFound
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=

feature6_void_to_typed.json.description: Implicitly convert a no-value result that is in a failure state into a value-carrying result of an arbitrary value type. The conversion preserves the failure status and copies across the failure payload: error messages for an error result, validation errors for an invalid result, and the bare status for not-found, forbidden and unauthorized results. The converted result carries no value.

cases.expected_output:
status=Error
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=test1
validationErrors=

status=Invalid
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=identifier=name;message=Name is required;code=;severity=Error|identifier=postalCode;message=PostalCode cannot exceed 10 characters;code=;severity=Error

feature7_paged_construction.json.description: Build a paginated result that pairs a value-carrying result with paging metadata (page number, page size, total pages, total records), then observe both the underlying result state and the paging metadata. Constructing a paginated result directly from a value yields a successful status carrying that value (including null) alongside the paging metadata. Converting an existing result into a paginated result preserves the original status and payload: a successful source carries its value; an error source carries its error messages; an invalid source carries its validation errors; not-found and forbidden sources carry their statuses. In every case the paging metadata is attached unchanged.

cases.expected_output:
status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=test string
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=
pageNumber=0
pageSize=10
totalPages=1
totalRecords=3

status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=
pageNumber=0
pageSize=10
totalPages=1
totalRecords=3

status=Error
isSuccess=false
value=null
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=Something bad happened.
validationErrors=
pageNumber=0
pageSize=10
totalPages=1
totalRecords=3

feature8_paged_implicit_conversions.json.description: Convert a value (or a successful result) into a paginated result and back, observing the carried value and the attached paging metadata. Converting a bare value into a result and then into a paginated result yields a successful paginated result carrying that value (including null) plus the paging metadata; extracting the value from such a paginated result returns the carried value. Building the source result with a success message preserves the message through the conversion alongside the value and paging metadata.

cases.expected_output:
status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=test string
successMessage=
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=
pageNumber=0
pageSize=10
totalPages=1
totalRecords=3
value=test string

status=Ok
isSuccess=true
value=test string
successMessage=Success
correlationId=
errors=
validationErrors=
pageNumber=0
pageSize=10
totalPages=1
totalRecords=3
